content:
# Genesis Chapter 41 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 23v–25r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_041_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_041_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_041_page_003.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_041_page_004.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 39–42 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0043

---

**1** And it came to pass at the end of two full years that Pharaoh was dreaming, and behold, he was standing by the Nile.

**2** And behold, out of the Nile there came up seven cows, fine in appearance and fat in flesh, and they grazed in the reeds.

**3** And behold, seven other cows came up after them out of the Nile, ugly in appearance and thin in flesh, and they stood beside the cows on the bank of the Nile.

**4** And the ugly and thin-fleshed cows ate up the seven fine-appearing and fat cows. Then Pharaoh awoke.

**5** And he slept and dreamed a second time, and behold, seven ears of grain were coming up on a single stalk, fat and good.

**6** And behold, seven thin ears, scorched by the east wind, were sprouting up after them.

**7** And the seven thin ears swallowed up the seven fat and full ears. Then Pharaoh awoke, and behold — it was a dream.

**8** And it came to pass in the morning that his spirit was troubled, and he sent and called for all the magicians of Egypt and all its wise men. And Pharaoh recounted to them his dream, but there was no one who could interpret them to Pharaoh.

**9** Then the chief cupbearer spoke to Pharaoh, saying, "I remember my offenses today.

**10** Pharaoh was angry with his servants and put me under guard in the house of the captain of the guard — me and the chief baker.

**11** And we dreamed a dream in one night, I and he; each according to the interpretation of his dream we dreamed.

**12** And there with us was a young man, a Hebrew, a servant of the captain of the guard. And we told him, and he interpreted our dreams to us — each according to his dream he interpreted.

**13** And it came to pass just as he interpreted to us, so it was: I was restored to my position and he was hanged."

**14** And Pharaoh sent and called for Joseph, and they brought him quickly from the pit. And he shaved himself and changed his garments and came to Pharaoh.

**15** And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I have dreamed a dream, and there is no one to interpret it. But I have heard it said of you that you hear a dream and can interpret it."

**16** And Joseph answered Pharaoh, saying, "It is not in me — God will answer Pharaoh's welfare."

**17** Then Pharaoh spoke to Joseph: "In my dream, behold, I was standing on the bank of the Nile.

**18** And behold, out of the Nile there came up seven cows, fat in flesh and fine in form, and they grazed in the reeds.

**19** And behold, seven other cows came up after them, wretched and very ugly in form and thin in flesh — I had never seen such as these in all the land of Egypt for ugliness.

**20** And the thin and ugly cows ate up the first seven fat cows.

**21** And they entered into their midst, and it could not be known that they had entered into their midst, for their appearance was as ugly as at the beginning. Then I awoke.

**22** And I saw in my dream, and behold, seven ears were coming up on a single stalk, full and good.

**23** And behold, seven ears, withered, thin, scorched by the east wind, were sprouting up after them.

**24** And the thin ears swallowed up the seven good ears. And I told it to the magicians, but there was no one who could explain it to me."

**25** And Joseph said to Pharaoh, "The dream of Pharaoh is one — what God is about to do, he has declared to Pharaoh.

**26** The seven good cows are seven years, and the seven good ears are seven years — the dream is one.

**27** And the seven thin and ugly cows that came up after them are seven years, and the seven empty ears scorched by the east wind shall be seven years of famine.

**28** It is the word that I spoke to Pharaoh — what God is about to do, he has shown to Pharaoh.

**29** Behold, seven years are coming: a great abundance throughout all the land of Egypt.

**30** And seven years of famine will arise after them, and all the abundance will be forgotten in the land of Egypt, and the famine will consume the land.

**31** And the abundance will not be known in the land because of that famine which follows, for it will be very severe.

**32** And the doubling of the dream to Pharaoh twice — it is because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it about.

**33** And now let Pharaoh seek out a man discerning and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt.

**34** Let Pharaoh do this, and let him appoint overseers over the land and take one-fifth of the land of Egypt in the seven years of abundance.

**35** And let them gather all the food of these good years that are coming, and let them pile up grain under the hand of Pharaoh — food in the cities — and let them keep it.

**36** And the food shall be a reserve for the land against the seven years of famine that shall be in the land of Egypt, so that the land may not perish through the famine."

**37** And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh and in the eyes of all his servants.

**38** And Pharaoh said to his servants, "Can we find such a one as this — a man in whom is the spirit of God?"

**39** And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "Since God has made all this known to you, there is no one discerning and wise as you.

**40** You shall be over my house, and by your word all my people shall be directed — only in the throne shall I be greater than you."

**41** And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "See, I have set you over all the land of Egypt."

**42** And Pharaoh removed his signet ring from his hand and put it on Joseph's hand, and clothed him in garments of fine linen, and placed the gold chain around his neck.

**43** And he had him ride in the second chariot that he had, and they called out before him: "Avrekh!" And he set him over all the land of Egypt.

**44** And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I am Pharaoh, but without you no man shall lift up his hand or his foot in all the land of Egypt."

**45** And Pharaoh called Joseph's name Zaphenath-paneah, and he gave him Asenath the daughter of Poti-phera, priest of On, as a wife. And Joseph went out over the land of Egypt.

**46** And Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh king of Egypt. And Joseph went out from before Pharaoh and passed through all the land of Egypt.

**47** And the land produced in the seven years of abundance by handfuls.

**48** And he gathered all the food of the seven years that were in the land of Egypt, and placed the food in the cities — the food of the fields of the city that surrounded it he placed within it.

**49** And Joseph piled up grain like the sand of the sea — exceedingly much — until he ceased counting, for it was beyond number.

**50** And to Joseph were born two sons before the year of famine came, whom Asenath the daughter of Poti-phera, priest of On, bore to him.

**51** And Joseph called the name of the firstborn Manasseh, "for God has made me forget all my hardship and all my father's house."

**52** And the name of the second he called Ephraim, "for God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction."

**53** And the seven years of abundance that were in the land of Egypt came to an end.

**54** And the seven years of famine began to come, just as Joseph had said. And there was famine in all lands, but in all the land of Egypt there was bread.

**55** And all the land of Egypt was hungry, and the people cried out to Pharaoh for bread. And Pharaoh said to all Egypt, "Go to Joseph; what he says to you, do."

**56** And the famine was over all the face of the earth. And Joseph opened all that was in them and sold to Egypt. And the famine was severe in the land of Egypt.

**57** And all the earth came to Egypt to Joseph to buy grain, for the famine was severe over all the earth.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four Leningrad Codex manuscript images and four Vaticanus manuscript images.

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 41 spans Folios 23v–25r across four manuscript images.

   **Folio 23v** (GEN_041_page_001.jpg): This is PDF page 45, the verso of Folio 23, photographed in inverted orientation. The image carries the conclusion of Genesis 40 (vv. 21–23 visible at the top of the folio in inverted form) followed immediately by the beginning of Genesis 41. The opening line of chapter 41 — וַיְהִי מִקֵּץ שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים ("and it came to pass at the end of two full years") — is clearly legible after the parashah break. The vocalization of מִקֵּץ (miqqets — "at the end of, after the end of") is fully visible, as is the full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation throughout. The chapter progresses through Pharaoh's dream of the seven fat and seven lean cows (vv. 1–4), the dream of the seven full and seven thin ears of grain (vv. 5–7), the troubled spirit of Pharaoh and the failure of the chartummim (vv. 8), and the cupbearer's sudden recollection of Joseph (vv. 9–13). The key phrase מִן הַבּוֹר (min ha-bor — "from the pit") in verse 14 is legible, completing the bor trajectory that began in Genesis 37. The folio carries the chapter to approximately verse 14 and the beginning of Joseph's response to Pharaoh.

   **Folio 24r** (GEN_041_page_002.jpg): This is PDF page 46, the recto of Folio 24. The three-column layout with full vocalization is clearly visible throughout, photographed right-side up. This folio carries Joseph's response to Pharaoh ("It is not in me — God will answer," v. 16), Pharaoh's extended recounting of both dreams (vv. 17–24), and Joseph's full interpretation — the seven years of abundance and seven years of famine (vv. 25–32), together with Joseph's practical counsel to appoint a wise overseer and gather grain (vv. 33–36). The key phrase בִּלְבַּד הַכִּסֵּא אֶגְדַּל מִמֶּךָּ (bilbad ha-kisse' egdal mimekka — "only in the throne shall I be greater than you," v. 40) is legible. The folio also carries the appointment scene through approximately verse 38, with the pivotal phrase רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (ruach Elohim — "the spirit of God," v. 38) clearly visible.

   **Folio 24v** (GEN_041_page_003.jpg): This is PDF page 47, the verso of Folio 24, photographed in inverted orientation. The folio carries the formal elevation of Joseph (vv. 39–52) — the investiture with the signet ring (תַּבַּעַת — taba'at), fine linen, and gold chain (vv. 41–42); the chariot ride and the call of אַבְרֵךְ (avrekh, v. 43); Joseph's authority declared by Pharaoh (v. 44); the Egyptian name צָפְנַת פַּעְנֵחַ (Zaphenath-paneah) and the wife Asenath (v. 45); Joseph's age at thirty (v. 46); the gathering of grain (vv. 47–49); and the births and naming of the two sons, מְנַשֶּׁה (Manasseh, v. 51) and אֶפְרַיִם (Ephraim, v. 52). The naming etymologies are fully legible: נַשַּׁנִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת כָּל עֲמָלִי ("God has made me forget all my hardship") for Manasseh and הִפְרַנִי אֱלֹהִים בְּאֶרֶץ עָנְיִי ("God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction") for Ephraim.

   **Folio 25r** (GEN_041_page_004.jpg): This is PDF page 48, the recto of Folio 25. The folio carries the conclusion of Genesis 41 (vv. 53–57) followed by the opening of Genesis 42. The three columns are clearly legible, showing the end of the seven years of abundance (v. 53), the beginning of the seven years of famine (vv. 54–57), and the worldwide extent of the famine — כָּל הָאָרֶץ בָּאוּ מִצְרַיְמָה ("all the earth came to Egypt," v. 57) — closing the chapter on a universal scale. The opening verses of Genesis 42 (vv. 1–2) are visible at the bottom of the folio.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Four Vaticanus pages were read directly.

   **Page 39** (GEN_041_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 39 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0043). This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 40 and the beginning of Genesis 41. The chapter notation "CAP XLVI" is visible in the right margin, marking the Vaticanus chapter boundary for Genesis 41. The Greek text begins Genesis 41 with the opening verse rendered in continuous uncial script. The LXX renders מִקֵּץ שְׁנָτַיִם יָמִים as Μετὰ δὲ τὴν δύο ἔτη ἡμερῶν ("and after two years of days") — preserving the compound phrase יָמִים (yamim — "days") alongside the year count. The page carries the chapter through the dreams and the beginning of the cupbearer's recollection.

   **Page 40** (GEN_041_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 40 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0044). This page carries the cupbearer's testimony, the summoning of Joseph from the pit (λάκκος), and Joseph's interpretation of Pharaoh's dreams. The key theological statement of verse 16 — οὐκ ἄνευ τοῦ θεοῦ ἀποκριθήσεται τὸ σωτήριον Φαραω ("not without God will there be an answer for Pharaoh's welfare") — is visible. The page carries the Joseph narrative through Joseph's practical counsel (vv. 33–36) and the appointment scene.

   **Page 41** (GEN_041_vaticanus_page_003.jpg): Vaticanus page 41 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0045). The chapter notation "CAT XLVI" is visible in the upper left margin, confirming the chapter boundary. This page carries the formal elevation of Joseph — the signet ring (δακτύλιος — daktylios), the fine linen robe, the gold chain (στρεπτὸν χρυσοῦν — a twisted/woven gold necklace), the second chariot, and the cry of κῆρυξ ἔμπροσθεν αὐτοῦ ("herald before him") at verse 43. The name Zaphenath-paneah and the wife Asenath are legible. This page carries the chapter through the grain-gathering and the naming of the sons (vv. 39–52).

   **Page 42** (GEN_041_vaticanus_page_004.jpg): Vaticanus page 42 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0046). This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 41 (vv. 53–57) and the beginning of Genesis 42. The closing verse of the chapter — πᾶσα δὲ ἡ γῆ ἦλθεν εἰς Αἴγυπτον ἀγοράσαι πρὸς Ιωσηφ ("and all the earth came to Egypt to buy from Joseph") — is visible, confirming the chapter's closing on the universal scope of the famine.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "At the end of two full years" — מִקֵּץ שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים (miqqets shnatayim yamim) (v. 1)**

The chapter opens with a temporal marker that resolves the two-year suspension created by the cupbearer's forgetting (40:23). The preposition-noun compound מִקֵּץ (miqqets) — from the root קָצָה (qatsah — "to cut off, to reach the end of") with the preposition מִן (min — "from") — means "from/at the end of, after the conclusion of." The compound שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים (shnatayim yamim — "two years, days") is a dual form of שָׁנָה (shanah — "year") combined with יָמִים (yamim — "days"). The yamim here is an appositional intensifier — "two years, each being a full set of days," i.e., "two full years." The phrase emphasizes the completeness and duration of the waiting period: not merely two years in the abstract but two years lived through day by day. Joseph was not sleeping — he was waiting, day after day, for the cupbearer's promise to be fulfilled, and it never came. Two complete years of this.

The word מִקֵּץ (miqqets) is not a common word; its most theologically resonant later occurrence is in Daniel 12:13 (קֵץ הַיָּמִין — "the end of the days"), where it functions as an eschatological terminus. In the Joseph narrative it is more mundane — a temporal hinge — but it carries the same force of "the end has now been reached; the appointed time has arrived." The two years that have passed are not an interruption of providence but the appointed duration of Joseph's confinement before the next stage unfolds.

The LXX renders Μετὰ δὲ τὴν δύο ἔτη ἡμερῶν ("but after the two years of days") — preserving the yamim as ἡμερῶν ("of days") and the temporal preposition as μετά ("after"). The formal translation renders "at the end of two full years" — preserving the miqqets as temporal terminus and yamim as the completeness intensifier ("full years").

**2. "The magicians" — הַחַרְטֻמִּים (ha-chartummim) (vv. 8, 24)**

The failure of the magicians is the pivot on which the whole narrative turns: Pharaoh's dreams are inexplicable to Egypt's professional interpreters, creating the vacuum into which Joseph will step. The term חַרְטֻמִּים (chartummim — "magicians, scribal-priests, ritual specialists") is an Egyptian loanword in Hebrew, appearing in the Bible only in the Joseph story and the Exodus plague narrative (Exodus 7–9). The word derives from Egyptian ḥr-tp or ḥry-tp — "chief of the head/lector-priest" or "one who recites from the scroll" — designating the learned priests who maintained the sacred scribal arts, interpreted portents, and performed ritual texts. They were Egypt's most sophisticated professional interpreters.

The irony is pointed: these are the men whose entire professional identity is the interpretation of dreams, signs, and divine communications — yet they cannot interpret Pharaoh's dreams. The Hebrew text underscores their failure with the blunt phrase אֵין פּוֹתֵר אוֹתָם לְפַרְעֹה (ein poter otam l'Far'oh — "there was no one interpreting them to Pharaoh"). The root פָּתַר (patar — "to interpret") connects directly to the pitron (interpretation) of chapters 40–41. The chartummim, Egypt's professional pitron-specialists, fail at the very task that defines them. Joseph — a Hebrew prisoner, not an Egyptian priest — succeeds.

The LXX renders χαρτουμμίν with a Greek transliteration-derived form, here ἐξηγηταί ("interpreters, expounders") in some manuscripts, though Vaticanus uses a form closer to the Hebrew loanword. The formal translation renders "magicians" — the established English equivalent for chartummim in the biblical context, which signals their priestly-ritual function as professional interpreters of divine communications.

**3. "It is not in me — God will answer Pharaoh's welfare" — בִּלְעָדָי אֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶה אֶת שְׁלוֹם פַּרְעֹה (bil'adai Elohim ya'aneh et shelom Far'oh) (v. 16)**

Joseph's response to Pharaoh's request in verse 15 is among the most theologically compressed sentences in the entire Joseph narrative. Four elements demand analysis.

First, בִּלְעָדָי (bil'adai) — "apart from me, not in me, without me." The preposition בְּ (be — "in/with") combined with לְעַד (le'ad — "apart from, without") creates a phrase of categorical self-exclusion: the interpretive capacity is not located in Joseph. This echoes the theological claim of 40:8 — "interpretations belong to God" — but now in a more urgent register. Where 40:8 was a general theological principle (interpretations belong to God, so tell me), 41:16 is a direct personal disclaimer: "do not look in me for this; it is not here."

Second, אֱלֹהִים (Elohim — "God") is the subject of the answering verb — emphatically placed as the one who will act. The divine name is not modified or hedged: it is the simple, universal name for the divine. Joseph is speaking to Pharaoh — a polytheist — using the generic divine name, as he did in 40:8. He does not say "my God" (as in "the God of my fathers") but simply "God" — available, universally comprehensible, unmistakable. The God who is the source of interpretations (40:8) is now the one who will answer.

Third, יַעֲנֶה (ya'aneh) — Qal imperfect third masculine singular of עָנָה (anah — "to answer, to respond, to testify"). This is the word for answering a question or testifying in a legal proceeding. Joseph is saying: God will give an answer to Pharaoh — God will speak through this encounter. The dream is Pharaoh's question to the divine realm; the interpretation is God's answer. Joseph is the medium, not the message.

Fourth, שְׁלוֹם פַּרְעֹה (shelom Far'oh — "the welfare/peace/well-being of Pharaoh"). The word שָׁלוֹם (shalom) here is not the conventional greeting but the substantive term for well-being, wholeness, and welfare — carrying the full freight of its semantic range. The answer God will give concerns Pharaoh's welfare — the interpretation will tell Pharaoh what he needs to know for the welfare of his kingdom. The LXX renders τὸ σωτήριον Φαραω ("the salvation/welfare of Pharaoh") — using σωτήριον (soteria), which in Greek carries the sense of "what makes safe, what brings deliverance/salvation." The LXX rendering is not wrong but imports a slightly weightier theological register than the MT's shalom. The formal translation renders "Pharaoh's welfare" — choosing the most common English semantic equivalent for shalom in this practical context.

**4. "The spirit of God" — רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (ruach Elohim) (v. 38)**

Pharaoh's rhetorical question in verse 38 — הֲנִמְצָא כָזֶה אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים בּוֹ ("can we find such a man as this, in whom is the spirit of God?") — is one of the most striking theological statements in Genesis. The pagan king of Egypt, using the universal divine name Elohim, attributes Joseph's wisdom to the indwelling of the divine spirit. This is not Joseph speaking about his own gifts; this is Pharaoh — a man who serves the gods of Egypt — testifying that the God (Elohim) is in Joseph.

The phrase רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (ruach Elohim) is the same phrase used in Genesis 1:2, where "the spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters" at the moment of creation. It appears throughout the Old Testament as the designation for the divine enabling presence — the spirit of God that comes upon judges (Judges 3:10, 6:34), prophets (Ezekiel 11:24), craftsmen (Exodus 31:3, where Bezalel is filled with ruach Elohim to make the tabernacle), and kings (1 Samuel 10:10, 16:13). The phrase thus identifies Joseph within a long tradition of Spirit-gifted leaders — but here the identification is made not by Israel's narrator but by Egypt's king.

The phrase ruach Elohim differs from ruach YHWH ("the spirit of the LORD") — the latter is more specifically the covenant name of Israel's God. Elohim is the universal divine name — the spirit that Pharaoh recognizes in Joseph is identified with the ultimate divine reality, whatever Pharaoh may call him. The Joseph narrative is careful about this: Joseph uses Elohim when speaking to Egyptians; the narrative uses YHWH when describing the divine presence with Joseph (39:2–5, 21, 23). Pharaoh's testimony with Elohim fits this pattern precisely — he recognizes the divine reality without naming the God of Israel by his covenant name.

The LXX renders πνεῦμα θεοῦ (pneuma theou — "spirit of God") — a direct equivalence. The formal translation renders "the spirit of God" — preserving the exact parallel with Genesis 1:2 and the long tradition of Spirit-endowment.

**5. "Avrekh" — אַבְרֵךְ (avrekh) (v. 43)**

The investiture of Joseph reaches its public climax in the chariot scene: Pharaoh has Joseph ride in the second royal chariot, and people call out before him אַבְרֵךְ (avrekh). This word has resisted a definitive etymology for centuries, and several proposals remain in scholarly circulation:

1. **Hebrew etymology: "bow the knee"** — taken as a compound of אַב (av — "father"?) and the imperative of בָּרַךְ (barakh — "to kneel, to bless"), yielding something like "bow down!" or "on your knees!" This would make avrekh a Hebrew command to prostrate before the advancing official.

2. **Egyptian etymology: "attention!"** — from Egyptian ib-rk ("attention, heart to you") or a related form used in royal processions. This is supported by the Egyptian context (the chariot procession, the Egyptian officials calling out) and by the proposed parallel with Egyptian texts that describe heralds crying titles before royal chariots.

3. **Akkadian etymology: abarakku** — a Mesopotamian court title for a high official, a steward or major-domo. This would make avrekh a cognate title rather than a command — "the abarakku!" proclaiming Joseph's new rank.

The narrator's deployment of an untranslated word here is itself significant. Unlike the Egyptian names Zaphenath-paneah and Asenath, which are given without gloss, avrekh is placed in the text as a sound — the sound of the crowd calling before Joseph's chariot. Whether command or title, it is the voice of Egypt acknowledging Joseph's elevation. The LXX renders κῆρυξ ἔμπροσθεν αὐτοῦ ("a herald before him") — apparently avoiding the term's difficulty by describing the action (a herald proclaiming) rather than reproducing the word. The formal translation preserves "Avrekh" — the MT's untranslated word — reflecting the narrator's own choice to leave it as a foreign sound in the Hebrew text, with a brief note explaining the debate.

**6. "Zaphenath-paneah" — צָפְנַת פַּעְנֵחַ (Tsafnat Pa'neach) (v. 45)**

Pharaoh's naming of Joseph with an Egyptian name — צָפְנַת פַּעְנֵחַ (Tsafnat Pa'neach) — is the formal integration of Joseph into the Egyptian court. Like the investiture with the signet ring, fine linen, and gold chain, the giving of an Egyptian name completes Joseph's official identity as a court official of Egypt. The name's meaning is disputed:

1. **"The one who reveals hidden things"** — a popular rabbinic and medieval interpretation, based on an assumed Egyptian etymology meaning "the god speaks and lives" or "he who deciphers the hidden things" — thematically apt for a dream interpreter.

2. **Egyptian: djed-p-ntr-iw.f-'ankh** — proposed reconstruction: "the god speaks; he lives" (Ranke, Vergote). This yields the sense that Joseph receives a divine title marking him as one through whom a god speaks.

3. **Egyptian: djad-pa-nute-ankh** — "he who is called, and lives" — or related forms. Scholarly consensus has not settled on a single reconstruction.

The LXX renders the name as Ψονθομφανήχ (Psonthomphanech) — a different transliteration reflecting a different Egyptian oral tradition. The formal translation preserves "Zaphenath-paneah" — the standard English rendering of the MT's Hebrew transliteration — without attempting to reconstruct the Egyptian original.

**7. "Manasseh" and "Ephraim" — naming etymologies (vv. 51–52)**

The births and naming of Joseph's two sons form the emotional and theological climax of his Egyptian elevation. Before the famine — before the narrative turns dark again — Joseph is given two sons and their names tell his story.

**Manasseh (מְנַשֶּׁה, Menasheh)**: "for God has made me forget (נַשַּׁנִי — nashani) all my hardship and all my father's house." The name is connected etymologically to the root נָשָׁה (nashah — "to forget, to cause to forget"), in the Piel/Hiphil causative sense: "God has caused me to forget." The root is unusual and the connection is phonological rather than perfectly etymological — מְנַשֶּׁה does not derive directly from nashah but the name carries the nashah sound. Joseph names his firstborn from the experience of forgetting — all the hardship (עֲמָלִי — amali, "my toil, my trouble, my suffering") and the father's house (בֵּית אָבִי — beit avi). This is not a repudiation of family or homeland — it is a testimony to the grace of God in enabling healing. The man who could not forget (Joseph never forgot; he wept, 43:30, 45:2) names his son from the gift of being able to set down the weight of his suffering. The naming is prospective and grateful, not a betrayal of origin.

The mention of "all my father's house" is tonally complex. Joseph has been separated from his father Jacob for approximately thirteen years by this point (sold at roughly seventeen, standing before Pharaoh at thirty, v. 46). He does not know whether Jacob is alive. The name Manasseh expresses both the healing God has given and the distance — it is the name of a man who has learned to live with what cannot be retrieved, trusting God with what he cannot control.

**Ephraim (אֶפְרַיִם, Efrayim)**: "for God has made me fruitful (הִפְרַנִי — hifreni, from פָּרָה — parah, 'to be fruitful, to bear fruit') in the land of my affliction (עָנְיִי — onyi, 'my affliction, my suffering, my poverty')." The name contains the sound of פָּרָה (parah — "fruitfulness") and is in a dual or plural form (the -ayim ending) suggesting abundance. The phrase "land of my affliction" is remarkable: Joseph does not rename Egypt "the land of my exaltation" or "the land of my prosperity" — the land that has been his place of suffering retains its identity as the land of affliction. But God has brought fruitfulness within it. The grace is not the erasure of the affliction but fruitfulness within and despite it.

The two names together tell a double truth about God's grace in suffering: Manasseh (God-given forgetting of the pain) and Ephraim (God-given fruitfulness within the painful land). They are not competing claims — they describe the two faces of redemptive providence. The order matters: first forgetting comes (Manasseh), then fruitfulness within the place of affliction (Ephraim). Jacob's later reversal of the birth-order blessing — giving the right hand to Ephraim, the second-born — will reorder these in Genesis 48, giving the precedence to fruitfulness over forgetting.

**8. "The signet ring" — הַתַּבַּעַת (ha-taba'at) (v. 42)**

Pharaoh removes his signet ring (תַּבַּעַת — taba'at) and places it on Joseph's hand. The taba'at in the ancient Near East was a cylinder or stamp seal ring, worn on the finger or a cord, used to authenticate official documents by impression in clay or wax. To give one's signet ring to another was to delegate one's authority — documents sealed with that ring carried the king's own authorization. This is the highest form of delegated authority in the ancient world.

The word taba'at echoes the earlier use of the related term חוֹתָם (chotam — "signet, seal") in Genesis 38:18, where Judah gives his chotam and cord and staff to Tamar as a pledge. The two words occupy the same semantic domain (seals of personal identity and authority), and the echo is not accidental. In Genesis 38, the chotam proves Judah's identity and implicates him in his own narrative failure. In Genesis 41, the taba'at is given by Pharaoh to Joseph as a symbol of total authority. The instrument of personal identification becomes the instrument of sovereign delegation: Joseph, who once had nothing, now carries the king's own seal. The trajectory from stripped coat (37:23) to royal signet ring is complete.

The LXX renders δακτύλιον (daktylion — "ring, finger-ring") — the standard Greek term for a ring, not specifically a signet ring, though the context makes the signet function clear. The formal translation renders "signet ring" to preserve the official character of the taba'at.

**9. "What God is about to do, he has declared/shown to Pharaoh" — אֵת אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה הִגִּיד לְפַרְעֹה (et asher ha-Elohim oseh higgid l'Far'oh) (vv. 25, 28)**

The double statement in Joseph's interpretation — "what God is about to do, he has declared to Pharaoh" (v. 25) and "what God is about to do, he has shown to Pharaoh" (v. 28) — frames the entire interpretation within the theological claim that God is the communicating agent. The two verbs are different: הִגִּיד (higgid — Hiphil of נָגַד, nagad — "to tell, to declare, to make known") in verse 25, and הֶרְאָה (her'ah — Hiphil of רָאָה, ra'ah — "to show, to cause to see") in verse 28. The first emphasizes verbal disclosure (God has declared it), the second visual revelation (God has shown it). Together they affirm that Pharaoh's dreams are not random — they are divine communication. God has both told and shown what is coming. Joseph's role is the mediation of an already-communicated divine disclosure.

The use of הָאֱלֹהִים (ha-Elohim — "the God, God") with the definite article is notable: this is not "a god" but "the God" — the definite article insisting on the uniqueness or particularity of the divine agent. Joseph uses ha-Elohim (with the article) in verses 25, 28, 32, while also using Elohim (without the article) in verses 16, 25, 32. The article can function to intensify the reference or to specify a known divine figure. In the context of Joseph speaking to Pharaoh, ha-Elohim may function as a way of pointing to the specific God who is acting here — the God of Joseph — without naming him as YHWH.

**10. "The doubling of the dream" — וְעַל הִשָּׁנוֹת הַחֲלוֹם אֶל פַּרְעֹה פַּעֲמָיִם (ve-al hishanot ha-chalom el Far'oh pa'amayim) (v. 32)**

The theological climax of Joseph's interpretation is his explicit explanation of why the dream came twice: כִּי נָכוֹן הַדָּבָר מֵעִם הָאֱלֹהִים וּמְמַהֵר הָאֱלֹהִים לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ ("because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it about"). The doubling (hishanot — from שָׁנָה, shanah — "to repeat, to do twice") is a divine confirmation device. Two witnesses establish a matter in law (Deuteronomy 19:15); two dreams with the same meaning establish the divine inevitability of the outcome.

The phrase נָכוֹן הַדָּבָר מֵעִם הָאֱלֹהִים (nakhon ha-davar me'im ha-Elohim — "the thing is established by God / the thing is firm from with God") uses the Niphal of כּוּן (kun — "to be established, to be firm, to be prepared"), indicating divine certainty: the matter has been fixed. The preposition מֵעִם (me'im — "from with, from the side of") locates the establishment in the divine sphere — it comes from God's side, not Pharaoh's or Joseph's. The adverb מְמַהֵר (memaher — "hastening, quickly") adds temporal urgency: God will not merely do it; God will do it soon.

This verse is the first time in the Joseph narrative that a human character explicitly articulates the principle of divine determinism that has governed the entire narrative. Joseph's brothers threw him in the pit; God was at work. Potiphar's wife falsely accused him; God was at work. The cupbearer forgot him for two years; God was at work. Now Joseph names the pattern: the doubling of the dream means God has decided this and will act quickly. What looks like dream interpretation is actually prophetic declaration: Joseph is telling Pharaoh not merely what the dream means but what God has determined to do.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 41

Pages 39–42 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible throughout, with some water staining on pages 40–41 but with the core text recoverable. The chapter notation "CAP XLVI" is visible in the right margin of page 39, continuing from the Genesis 40 notation on the same page. Key variants and equivalences observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 41:1 | מִקֵּץ שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים ("at the end of two full years") | Μετὰ δὲ τὴν δύο ἔτη ἡμερῶν ("but after two years of days") | MT followed | LXX preserves yamim as ἡμερῶν ("of days"), recognizing the intensifying function of the phrase. The formal translation renders "two full years" to capture this function in English. |
| 41:2 | יְאֹר ("the Nile") | τοῦ ποταμοῦ ("the river") | MT: "the Nile" | MT uses יְאֹר (ye'or), the Hebrew loanword from Egyptian ꜥwy-rw ("the great river") — the specific name for the Nile. LXX renders generically as "the river." The formal translation renders "the Nile" to capture the specific Egyptian geographic referent. |
| 41:2 | בְּאַחוּ ("in the reeds/reed grass") | ἐν τῷ ἄχει ("in the reeds") | Equivalent | MT's אָחוּ (achu — Egyptian loanword for Nile reed grass) is rendered with the Greek ἄχει (a transliteration-derived form). Both identify the marshy reed growth along the Nile bank. |
| 41:3 | עַל שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר ("on the bank of the Nile") | ἐπὶ χείλους τοῦ ποταמοῦ ("on the lip of the river") | MT followed | LXX renders שָׂפָה (safah — "bank, lip, edge") with χεῖλος ("lip, edge, bank") — a direct equivalence. The formal translation renders "bank." |
| 41:8 | הַחַרְטֻמִּים ("the magicians") | τοὺς ἐξηγητάς ("the interpreters/expounders") | MT: "the magicians" | LXX renders chartummim as ἐξηγηταί ("expounders, interpreters") — emphasizing their interpretive function. The MT's chartummim is an Egyptian loanword designating priestly ritual specialists. The formal translation renders "magicians" as the established English term for this Egyptian priestly caste. |
| 41:13 | כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר לָנוּ כֵּן הָיָה ("just as he interpreted to us, so it was") | καθάπερ συνέκρινεν ἡμῖν οὕτως καὶ συνέβη ("just as he judged/decided for us, so also it happened") | MT followed | LXX continues its pattern of rendering פָּתַר (patar — "to interpret") as συγκρίνω ("to judge, to decide"), the same divergence noted in Genesis 40:22. The MT's patar is the chapter's key word for dream-interpretation; the formal translation consistently renders it "interpret." |
| 41:14 | מִן הַבּוֹר ("from the pit") | ἐκ τοῦ λάκκου ("from the pit/cistern") | Equivalent | MT: min ha-bor; LXX: ek tou lakkou. Both terms — בּוֹר and λάκκος — are used consistently for Joseph's pit/prison throughout the narrative (37:20–28, 40:15, 41:14). The formal translation renders "from the pit" — preserving the bor verbal thread. |
| 41:16 | בִּלְעָדָי אֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶה אֶת שְׁלוֹם פַּרְעֹה ("it is not in me; God will answer Pharaoh's welfare") | οὐκ ἄνευ τοῦ θεοῦ ἀποκριθήσεται τὸ σωτήριον Φαραω ("not without God will there be an answer for Pharaoh's welfare") | MT followed | The LXX's οὐκ ἄνευ τοῦ θεοῦ ("not without God") inverts the MT's bil'adai ("not in me") — LXX makes God the indispensable agent rather than making Joseph's incapacity the starting point. Theologically equivalent but rhetorically different: MT foregrounds Joseph's self-negation; LXX foregrounds divine necessity. The MT's self-negation is formally more significant and is followed. |
| 41:25 | אֵת אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה הִגִּיד לְפַרְעֹה ("what God is about to do, he has declared to Pharaoh") | ὅσα ὁ θεὸς ποιεῖ ἔδειξεν τῷ Φαραω ("all that God does he has shown to Pharaoh") | MT followed | LXX uses ἔδειξεν ("showed") in v. 25 where MT has הִגִּיד (higgid — "declared/told"), and uses a form of δεικνύω also in v. 28. MT distinguishes the two verbs (higgid/"declared" in v. 25, her'ah/"shown" in v. 28). The formal translation preserves the MT's verbal distinction: "declared" (v. 25) and "shown" (v. 28). |
| 41:38 | רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים ("the spirit of God") | πνεῦμα θεοῦ ("spirit of God") | Equivalent | Direct equivalence: ruach Elohim = pneuma theou. Both designate the divine spirit-endowment of Joseph. |
| 41:43 | אַבְרֵךְ ("Avrekh!") | κῆρυξ ἔμπροσθεν αὐτοῦ ("a herald before him") | MT preserved | The LXX avoids the difficulty of the word avrekh by describing the action (a herald proclaiming before Joseph's chariot) rather than reproducing the term. This is a significant interpretive divergence. The MT's avrekh is preserved in the formal translation as an untranslated word, reflecting the narrator's own retention of the foreign term. |
| 41:45 | צָפְנַת פַּעְנֵחַ ("Zaphenath-paneah") | Ψονθομφανήχ ("Psonthomphanech") | MT form followed | The LXX's transliteration of Joseph's Egyptian name differs significantly from the MT's. Both represent attempts to render an Egyptian name in Semitic/Greek letters. The MT form "Zaphenath-paneah" is used throughout the formal translation as the standard rendering. |
| 41:45 | אָסְנַת בַּת פּוֹטִי פֶרַע ("Asenath the daughter of Poti-phera") | Ασενεθ θυγατέρα Πετεφρῆ ("Asenath daughter of Petephres") | Equivalent | The LXX renders Poti-phera as Πετεφρῆ (Petephres) — a slight variation in the Greek transliteration of the Egyptian name. The underlying Egyptian name (pr-fy-R' — "he whom Re has given") is the same. The MT form "Poti-phera" is used. |
| 41:51 | נַשַּׁנִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת כָּל עֲמָלִי ("God has made me forget all my hardship") | ἐπελάθεσθαί με ὁ θεὸς πάντων τῶν πόνων μου ("God has caused me to forget all my labors/toils") | Equivalent | MT: nashani (Hiphil of nashah — "cause to forget") + amali ("my hardship/toil"). LXX: ἐπελάθεσθαι (infinitive of ἐπιλανθάνομαι — "to forget") + πόνων ("labors, toils, sufferings"). Both readings are equivalent. |
| 41:52 | בְּאֶרֶץ עָנְיִי ("in the land of my affliction") | ἐν γῇ ταπεινώσεώς μου ("in the land of my humiliation/lowering") | Equivalent | MT: onyi ("my affliction, my poverty, my suffering"). LXX: ταπεινώσεως ("humiliation, lowering, abasement"). The LXX's ταπείνωσις adds a note of social humiliation to the physical/material suffering of ענִי. Both capture the force of the MT's term. |
| 41:57 | כָּל הָאָרֶץ בָּאוּ מִצְרַיְמָה ("all the earth came to Egypt") | πᾶσα δὲ ἡ γῆ ἦλθεν εἰς Αἴγυπτον ("and all the earth came to Egypt") | Equivalent | Both MT and LXX close the chapter with the universal scope of the famine driving all nations to Joseph. |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Descent and Ascent**

Genesis 41 is the pivot of the entire Joseph narrative — the chapter that converts descent into ascent, pit into palace, slave into viceroy. The formal structure is carefully designed:

- **Vv. 1–7**: Pharaoh's two dreams (seven fat/seven lean cows; seven full/seven thin ears)
- **Vv. 8–13**: The failure of Egypt's interpreters; the cupbearer finally remembers
- **Vv. 14–16**: Joseph brought from the pit; the theological disclaimer
- **Vv. 17–24**: Pharaoh recounts the dreams
- **Vv. 25–32**: Joseph interprets — seven years of plenty, seven of famine
- **Vv. 33–36**: Joseph's unsolicited counsel — appoint an overseer, store grain
- **Vv. 37–44**: Pharaoh appoints Joseph over all Egypt
- **Vv. 45–49**: Joseph's investiture and administration begin
- **Vv. 50–52**: Two sons born — Manasseh and Ephraim
- **Vv. 53–57**: The seven years of abundance end; the seven years of famine begin

The chapter is architecturally symmetrical around the pivot of verses 37–44 (the appointment). Before the pivot: Pharaoh dreams and cannot understand (vv. 1–8); Joseph interprets (vv. 14–32); Joseph counsels (vv. 33–36). After the pivot: Pharaoh delegates everything to Joseph (vv. 39–44); Joseph administers (vv. 45–49); Joseph's family grows (vv. 50–52); Joseph's wisdom proves exactly right (vv. 53–57). The symmetry is that of prophecy and fulfillment: the interpretation is precise and the outcome is exactly what was predicted.

**The bor Resolved**

The word בּוֹר (bor — "pit") appears at the narrative's axis in verse 14: וַיִּשְׁלַח פַּרְעֹה וַיִּקְרָא אֶת יוֹסֵף וַיְרִיצֻהוּ מִן הַבּוֹר ("and Pharaoh sent and called for Joseph, and they brought him quickly from the pit"). This is the bor's final narrative appearance as a place of Joseph's confinement. The trajectory of the word across the Joseph narrative:

- **37:20, 22, 24, 28**: The wilderness pit — Joseph thrown in by his brothers
- **40:15**: Joseph calls the prison a bor — "they put me into the pit"
- **41:14**: Joseph brought up from the bor by Pharaoh's summons

The final emergence from the bor in 41:14 is deliberately rapid: וַיְרִיצֻהוּ (vayyerituhu — "and they caused him to run, they hurried him") — Joseph is not merely released, he is rushed out of the pit. The same abruptness that brought him down (37:24: וַיַּשְׁלִכוּ אֹתוֹ הַבֹּרָה — "and they threw him into the pit") now brings him up. The bor's power is ended by a single royal word. This is the narrative's enacted theology: the pit has no final authority. The LORD who was with Joseph in every pit (39:2, 21, 23) has now used a foreign king's need to bring Joseph out.

**Joseph's Theological Self-Presentation**

Three times in Genesis 41, Joseph attributes his capacities to God rather than to himself:

1. **V. 16**: "It is not in me — God will answer Pharaoh's welfare." (Self-negation; divine attribution)
2. **V. 25**: "What God is about to do, he has declared to Pharaoh." (Joseph as conduit of prior divine disclosure)
3. **V. 28**: "What God is about to do, he has shown to Pharaoh." (Repetition of the same claim)

This triple attribution is not rhetorical modesty — it is the operating theology of the entire Joseph narrative. Joseph's gifts (dream interpretation, administrative wisdom, providential foresight) are given by God and exercised in God's service. The pattern begun in 40:8 ("interpretations belong to God") is here brought to completion: Joseph stands before the most powerful ruler in the known world and consistently, in every relevant moment, points away from himself toward God.

The result is paradoxical: this persistent self-negation is precisely what elevates Joseph. Pharaoh, hearing Joseph attribute everything to God, concludes: "Can we find such a man as this, in whom is the spirit of God?" (v. 38). And then: "Since God has made all this known to you, there is no one discerning and wise as you" (v. 39). Joseph's theology is the basis of his promotion. The man who refuses to claim credit for what God has done is given authority over everything. The theological pattern is the same pattern articulated later in Proverbs 22:4 (the reward of humility and the fear of the LORD) and in the Sermon on the Mount ("blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth"). In Joseph, Genesis narrates the pattern before Proverbs names it.

**The Unsolicited Counsel (vv. 33–36)**

One of the most narratively subtle moments of the chapter is the transition from verse 32 to verse 33. Pharaoh has asked Joseph to interpret the dream; Joseph has interpreted it (vv. 25–32). At verse 32, the interpretation is complete. At verse 33, Joseph continues speaking — uninvited — to offer practical administrative counsel: "And now let Pharaoh seek out a man discerning and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt."

Joseph was not asked for advice. He was asked for interpretation. The interpretation is finished. But Joseph sees the need and speaks into it. This is the first moment in the narrative where Joseph operates as more than an interpreter — he functions as a counselor, a statesman, a man who not only understands what God has determined but who sees immediately what must be done in response. The counsel is practical, specific, and wise: appoint a single overseer (not a committee), set him over all Egypt (not just some regions), collect one-fifth during the years of abundance (a specific and reasonable levy), store the grain in the cities (a geographically distributed strategy). This is not vague advice — it is a functional administrative plan.

The narrator signals that this is what tips the scales: "And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh and in the eyes of all his servants" (v. 37). It is not merely the interpretation but the counsel that impresses. And Pharaoh's response is immediate: "Can we find such a man as this?" (v. 38) — not "such a man who can interpret dreams" but "such a man" full stop — a man in whom the spirit of God dwells and who combines divine insight with human wisdom.

**Joseph at Thirty: The Age of Service**

Verse 46 notes that Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh. This detail is not incidental. In the Israelite tradition, thirty was the age at which Levites entered full service at the tabernacle (Numbers 4:3), and it became associated with the beginning of significant public ministry. David became king at thirty (2 Samuel 5:4). The note that Joseph was thirty when his Egyptian ministry began positions him within a pattern that the narrative's later readers would recognize — the beginning of significant divine commission at the completion of the thirtieth year.

Joseph was sold at seventeen (Genesis 37:2) and stands before Pharaoh at thirty (v. 46). Thirteen years have passed from the pit of Dothan to the palace of Pharaoh — years spent in Potiphar's house, in the prison, in the pit. These thirteen years are the cost of the journey that Providence has required. The text does not soften this: thirteen years is a long time. Joseph was seventeen when his life was disrupted; he is thirty when it begins to be restored. The naming of Manasseh — "God has made me forget all my hardship" — takes on its full weight against this arithmetic.
